ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for lotus-leaf-wrapped glutinous rice parcels; FOODON_03315671 (glutinous rice food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers glutinous rice dishes broadly; no specific code exists for lotus-leaf-wrapped preparations in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific dim sum preparation; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard lotus-leaf parcel as served in Singaporean dim sum restaurants weighs approximately 200 g and provides around 438 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Glutinous Rice in Lotus Leaf — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Glutinous Rice in Lotus Leaf (CR ID F05100060). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for steamed glutinous rice with pork and sausage filling (USDA SR Legacy #21376 and component analysis). Iron reported as -1 in source (data not available in HPB record).

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — glutinous rice (sticky rice)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 98 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous (sticky) rice, which is the primary carbohydrate component of this dish. The lotus-leaf parcel preparation (steaming) does not materially alter the GI of glutinous rice. GL per serving calculated as: GI (98) × available carbohydrate per serving (29.0 g/100g × 200 g serving = 58.0 g) / 100 = 56.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 466 mg per 100 g, driven by soy sauce, oyster sauce, dried shrimp, and lap cheong
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; a single 200 g parcel delivers approximately 932 mg sodium — over 40% of the 2,000 mg daily limit — and the low potassium content (~150 mg/100 g estimated) provides insufficient offset to the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice GI ~98 with 29 g carbohydrates per 100 g and only 0.92 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; the very high glycaemic index of glutinous rice (~98) combined with minimal fibre (0.92 g/100 g) produces rapid and pronounced postprandial blood glucose elevation, and a standard 200 g serving carries a glycaemic load of approximately 57.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (466 mg/100 g), saturated fat from lap cheong and char siew, and refined carbohydrate load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly; the combination of high sodium, estimated saturated fat (~2.3 g/100 g from processed pork products), and a high glycaemic load creates a compounded cardiovascular risk profile, and regular consumption is inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium, pro-inflammatory processed meats (lap cheong, dried shrimp), and refined carbohydrate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise this dish; sodium at 466 mg/100 g worsens tissue oedema, processed meats contribute saturated fat and pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid, and glutinous rice as a refined carbohydrate amplifies systemic inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Glutinous Rice in Lotus Leaf (荷叶糯米鸡, Lo Mai Gai) is a classic Cantonese dim sum dish widely consumed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. It consists of seasoned glutinous (sticky) rice stuffed with savoury fillings — typically sliced lap cheong (Chinese wax sausage), diced char siew (Cantonese barbecued pork), dried shrimp, and shiitake mushrooms — wrapped tightly in dried lotus leaves and steamed until the rice is fully cooked and fragrant. The lotus leaf imparts a distinctive earthy, slightly floral aroma to the rice during steaming. The texture is dense, sticky, and moist, with a rich umami flavour from the soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil marinade. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 219 kcal, 9.69 g protein, 7.1 g fat, and 29 g carbohydrates with only 0.92 g dietary fibre. Sodium is high at 466 mg/100 g. The glutinous rice has a very high glycaemic index (~98), making this dish a high-glycaemic-load food unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. A standard serving is one parcel (~200 g), delivering approximately 438 kcal and 932 mg sodium. The dish is a staple of yum cha (dim sum brunch) culture and is also commonly eaten as a breakfast item in Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100060), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous entry and documented. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous rice. Iron reported as -1 per HPB source (data not available).
notes: iron_mg is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo source record for F05100060 does not report an iron value for this preparation; limited published data exists for this specific traditional dim sum item in the HPB database as of 2026. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional lotus-leaf-wrapped preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Saturated fat (2.3 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous steamed glutinous rice with pork sausage filling and is not directly reported by HPB; this value should be verified against a laboratory analysis if available. Halal status is marked false as lap cheong (Chinese pork sausage) is a pork product.
